preFix.title: Spencerian Minuscule: Oval Group
preFix.subtitle: Pointed pen oval letters at 52 degree slant, shading on the down stroke only
preFix.excerpt: The oval group (o, a, d, g, q, p, b) is the structural core of Spencerian script. All are built from the same fundamental oval form written with a pointed pen held at nearly horizontal, shading applied only on the down stroke by the lightest pressure increase.
preFix.sourceNotes: Spencer, Platt Rogers. Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship. New York: Ivison, Blakeman, Taylor, 1866. Oval principles and letter construction.Historical context: nineteenth century

content.text: Platt Rogers Spencer created Spencerian script in the mid-as a fast, clear, graceful hand for American business. Its most striking quality is the hairline up stroke combined with a gently shaded down stroke. Both made with a pointed flexible nib. The Spencerian oval is the key form in this script. Write it well and the letters a, d, g, q, p, and b all follow from it.
Equipment

A Hunt 101 nib is the standard choice for Spencerian practice: it is flexible enough to give noticeable shading with very light pressure but springy enough to return to a hairline easily. Fit it into an oblique pen holder. The oblique flange offsets the nib to the right. Which naturally produces the correct 52 degree slant without awkward wrist rotation. Use black writing ink (not india ink, which is too thick and will clog the fine tines).
Pen angle

The pen is held almost horizontal: the barrel sits at 5 to 7 degrees above the writing surface. This is the defining posture of pointed-pen scripts and is very different from the broad-edge pen's near-vertical hold. The near-horizontal angle means the nib tines spring open with minimal downward pressure, producing a fine, controllable shade. Hold the pen barrel lightly between the first two fingers and the thumb with the hand resting on the last two fingers.
The shade rule

In Spencerian script, shade occurs only on the down stroke, never on the up stroke. This is an absolute rule. Any upward-travelling stroke is a hairline. The degree of shade in Spencerian is subtle: Spencer described it as a slight swelling of the stroke. Not a dramatic thick-thin contrast. Pressing too hard produces copperplate-weight shading, which is too heavy for authentic Spencerian.
Ruling up for Spencerian

Spencerian is written on a grid of horizontal lines and slant lines. The body height is about 3 mm for practice. Slant lines run at 52 degrees from the horizontal. Rule a set of these in pencil before beginning: the discipline of writing to the slant lines is essential and develops the consistent oval tilt.
The fundamental oval

The Spencerian oval is written in two strokes. Stroke 1: begin at the top of the oval. Come over the top with a hairline curve and descend down the left side. Adding shade as the stroke reaches its maximum leftward lean, then continue down and around the base. Stroke 2: from the bottom of the oval. A hairline curves upward on the right side, over the top. And closes the oval at the entry point.
The shade occurs only on the left descending portion of stroke 1. The right side of the oval and the full length of stroke 2 are hairlines.
Letter o

attrs.definition: Stroke 1 (overturn): hairline entry from top, curve left and descend with a gentle shade on the left side, round the base as a hairline. Stroke 2 (underturn): hairline up the right side, over the top to close at the entry point. Oval tilts at 52 degrees. Shade on the left descending portion of stroke 1 only. x-height 3 mm.

content.text: Letter a

Spencerian 'a' is the oval with a stem added on the right side. Write the oval first. Then continue stroke 2 past the closing point: carry the hairline upward to the waistline. Then pull down as a shaded descending stroke. Rounding at the base into a hairline exit to the right. The oval and the stem share the same slant angle.
Letter d

Spencerian 'd' adds an ascending stem to the oval. Write the oval. After closing. Continue the hairline upward past the waistline to the ascender height. Forming a loop at the top of the ascender. Then pull back down as a shaded descending stroke that merges with the right side of the oval. The loop at the top is slight: it is a small oval at the head of the ascender. Not a large cursive loop.
Practice sequence
Write rows of the oval alone at a slow pace, counting strokes. Focus on keeping stroke 2 a pure hairline with no pressure.
Write o, o, o with a connecting hairline between each: the exit hairline of one oval connects directly into the entry of the next.
Write a, a, a connected, then d, d, d, checking slant lines each time.

items.fix: Release all pressure at the very bottom of each curve before beginning the upward movement. Consciously lighten the grip for every upward stroke.
items.cause: Downward pressure is maintained through the turn at the base of the oval into the upward stroke.
items.symptom: Upward strokes are shaded instead of hairlines

items.fix: Rule the 52 degree slant lines and ensure the major axis of the oval aligns with them on every letter. Reduce writing speed until the slant is automatic.
items.cause: Not following the ruled slant guide lines.

items.fix: Check the nib under a magnifier: the tines should be perfectly level. Use a smoother paper surface (hot-press watercolour or good writing paper) if cartridge is snagging the tines.
items.cause: Nib tines not aligned, or nib is too stiff for the paper.
items.symptom: Nib catches and tears paper on up strokes

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.5 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Write o, o, o with a connecting hairline between each: the exit hairline of one oval connects directly into the entry of the next.
